name: DeepJudge
verdict: Technically-real institutional-knowledge retrieval layer that lawyers' agents actually plug into (Harvey, CoCounsel, MCP) — a credible picks-and-shovels KM infrastructure play, not a wrapper; main risk is its biggest partners absorbing the layer. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Lawstronaut
verdict: The most thesis-aligned of the cohort — a genuine agent-native legal-data API/MCP infra layer with credible founders — but earliest and thinnest on funding/traction evidence. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Syllo
verdict: The strongest of this batch: well-funded ($30M Venrock), litigator-founded, with real multi-LLM agentic document review proven at AmLaw scale — technically-real supervised autonomy, with the main open question being interop/API depth. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Ankar
verdict: Best-evidenced of the set: well-funded, agentic, citation-accountable patent OS with real legal-system integrations and named customers — strong thesis fit. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Moonlit.AI
verdict: Technically-real legal-data infrastructure play (API+MCP, authoritative EU corpus, marquee public-sector users) that fits the supervised-autonomy thesis well, but very early-stage and thinly funded. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Airia
verdict: Technically-real horizontal agentic platform with genuine MCP/orchestration depth and a credible operator-founder; 'legal' is a thin vertical skin and the moat + the self-funded round are the open questions. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Definely
verdict: Well-funded, well-distributed Word-native drafting/review tool moving credibly into agentic review (Enhance), but interop is closed embedding and the review space is crowding fast. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Leah (ex-ContractPodAi)
verdict: Mature, deeply interoperable CLM repositioning as an agentic 'OS' with a PwC alliance, but no new funding since 2021 and the rebrand reads as a reset under a crowded-category overhang. [PROVISIONAL]

name: August
verdict: Technically-real, well-integrated configurable drafting/review agent for the underserved midsize segment, strongly backed at seed. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Eudia
verdict: A credible, well-funded F500-focused agentic legal platform whose real edge is an AI-plus-acquired-human-ALSP roll-up — technically real and embedded where lawyers work, but defensibility and open interoperability are unproven. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Vesence
verdict: Sharp, well-backed YC agentic review-QA tool living in Word/Outlook with a strong early-usage signal, but very early, single-round, and no named customers or open interop yet. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Newcode.ai
verdict: Technically-real agentic player with a marquee Nordic anchor (DLA Piper) but early and integration depth unproven. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Pandektes
verdict: Differentiated cross-jurisdictional EU legal-research engine with a real API and a hard data moat thesis, but early, lightly funded, and thin on team/customer evidence. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Kuberno
verdict: Credibly funded (Nasdaq + Barclays), API-first entity-governance system of record adding supervised-autonomy AI (KAIA) — strong interoperability and accountability fit, though it sits in compliance-ops rather than core legal practice. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Wexler AI
verdict: Credible, well-backed UK litigation fact-intelligence play with a genuine agentic assistant and accountability-first design, but interop depth is unproven. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Structureflow
verdict: Distinctive, well-adopted legal visual-structuring tool with strong logos, but more a focused AI-assisted workspace than an agentic interop play. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Orbital
verdict: Credible, well-funded vertical real-estate legal AI with genuine accountability DNA (citations + its own regulated law firm), but interoperability is unproven and the engine sits on commodity Azure OpenAI — strong thesis fit, weak moat evidence. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Cicero (by Automatise)
verdict: Security-first private (self-hosted Llama) litigation analysis tool with a strong distribution partner in TransPerfect, but company financials and team depth are opaque. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Scissero
verdict: A credibly agentic, lawyers-in-the-loop legal-AI services firm whose open-source Suzie Law stack is the real (and unusually transparent) technical proof point — but it's a services-led, undisclosed-funding ALSP that grew via a distressed acquisition, with a deliberately weak software moat and no enterprise DMS integrations yet. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Truth Systems
verdict: Credible, well-backed AI-governance guardrail agent for law firms with real named traction; technically real but currently a browser-layer enforcement/audit play rather than deeply interoperable agentic infrastructure — watch for API/MCP/DMS depth. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Jigsaw
verdict: Differentiated diagramming-as-data play with strong backing and repeat founders, but closed integration surface and no agentic/interop depth limit thesis fit. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Crimson
verdict: Credible litigation-native founders and YC backing, but a tiny, very-early vertical LLM app with thin interop and self-reported traction. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Emma Legal
verdict: Very early but founder-strong Belgian M&A due-diligence AI with a real data-room integration; traction and moat still unproven. [PROVISIONAL]

name: PhaseLaw
verdict: Focused, well-built AI-native redaction/DSAR tool with a strong technical team, but narrow scope and standalone (low interop). [PROVISIONAL]

name: Aloi
verdict: Plausible firm-knowledge/contract-intelligence play with the right thesis framing, but thinly evidenced — founders, round and integrations unconfirmed. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Augmetec
verdict: Domain-credible investigations workflow AI with real DMS/ediscovery connectors, but very early, unfunded-deep, and 'command centre' agentic claims are unverified. [PROVISIONAL]

name: LUPL
verdict: Well-integrated legal matter/PM platform that lives where lawyers work, but funding looks stale and AI is a thin Copilot layer rather than agentic. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Casey
verdict: Credible solo-founder London bet on productizing law-firm expertise into client-facing AI agents, but funding/traction/interop are all unconfirmed and the name collides badly with an unrelated YC insurance 'Casey' — promising pitch, thin proof. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Awesome Compliance (ACT)
verdict: Early but thesis-aligned EU compliance startup: real multi-agent DPIA/ROPA drafting with human oversight, but pre-seed-thin on funding, traction and interoperability. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Mage Legal
verdict: Early YC transactional-diligence tool with source-cited outputs but thin funding/traction and weak interoperability; note it is diligence, not e-discovery. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Pivot (yourpivot)
verdict: Domain-credible UK family-law evidence tool with a nice knowledge-graph story but pre-seed, closed SaaS, and zero interoperability — interesting niche, not yet technically-real to the thesis. [PROVISIONAL]

name: Moritz
verdict: Legitimately funded, well-pedigreed AI-native law firm — but a services business with an internal AI tool, not the agentic/interoperable software the thesis rewards; off-thesis as a tech vendor. [PROVISIONAL]

name: KomplyAI
verdict: Thin-evidence AI-GRC workflow tool in a crowded compliance category; no funding/agentic/integration signals to support the thesis. [PROVISIONAL]
